coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of 3D CQDs/urchin-like and yolk-shell TiO2 hierarchical structure with enhanced photocatalytic properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhaoying
Wang, Qi
Shen, Tao
Hou, Xianjie
Kuang, Jianlei
Liu, Wenxiu
Cao, Wenbin
coredata.dc:description: A novel CQDs/TiO2 hierarchical structure with enhanced photocatalytic properties was achieved by uniformly decorating urchin-like and yolk-shell TiO2 microspheres (UYTMs) with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through an environmentally friendly hydrothermal process. The CQDs were firstly synthesized by the electrochemical method, and the TEM, Raman and PL characterizations strongly indicated that the as-prepared CQDs exhibited good dispersion, high crystallinity and unique up-conversion properties. The UYTMs synthesized by a NaOH-assisted hydrothermal process showed stable 3D hierarchical structure and large surface area, which was beneficial for light absorption and contacting with contamination. The good combination of CQDs and UYTMs was further successfully achieved during the hydrothermal process, and demonstrated by a series of tests. The photocatalytic experiments suggested that the CQDs/UYTMs exhibited better photocatalytic activities than the pure UYTMs and P25 under both visible and UV light irradiation. The CQDs/UYTMs combining with 6 wt% of CQDs showed the best photocatalytic efficiency, while excessive CQDs tended to inhibit the photocatalytic activity. According to the results and discussions, a possible mechanism in improving the photocatalytic efficiency of the CQDs/UYTMs is significantly proposed. The up-conversion property of CQDs can broaden the absorption spectrum of CQDs/UYTMs to the visible light. Moreover, the CQDs, as the electron reservoirs, are efficient to separate the electrons and holes, leading to an improved photocatalytic activity of CQDs/UYTMs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
TiO2
Urchin-like microspheres
Yolk shell
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs/UYTMs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation process of the CQDs/UYTMs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article)
TEM and HRTEM images of the prepared CQDs.
Photograph of the CQDs in water under (a) white and (b) UV light with wavelength at 365 nm irradiation. (c) the down-converted PL spectra and (d) the up-converted PL spectra of the CQDs in water.
SEM images of (a) the TiO2 precursor microspheres and (b) the UYTMs, TEM image of UYTMs with (c) low magnification and (d) high magnification.
High resolution TEM images of the CQDs/UYTMs.
XRD patterns of the CQDs/UYTMs and the UYTMs.
(a) XPS spectrum of CQDs/UYTMs sample and high resolution XPS spectra of CQDs/UYTMs: (b) C-1s, (c) O-1s, (d) Ti-2p.
PL spectra of CQDs/UYTMs composite and pure UYTMs.
The photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue over the CQDs/UYTMs, P25 and pure UYTMs under UV light irradiation.
The photocatalytic degradation of phenol over the CQDs/UYTMs with various content of CQDs, UYTMs and P25 under visible light irradiation.
Recyclability of 6 wt% of CQDs/UYTMs under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of 3D CQDs/urchin-like and yolk-shell TiO2 hierarchical structure with enhanced photocatalytic properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin College, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Tianjin 301830, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin College, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel CQDs/TiO2 hierarchical structure with enhanced photocatalytic properties was achieved by uniformly decorating urchin-like and yolk-shell TiO2 microspheres (UYTMs) with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through an environmentally friendly hydrothermal process. The CQDs were firstly synthesized by the electrochemical method, and the TEM, Raman and PL characterizations strongly indicated that the as-prepared CQDs exhibited good dispersion, high crystallinity and unique up-conversion properties. The UYTMs synthesized by a NaOH-assisted hydrothermal process showed stable 3D hierarchical structure and large surface area, which was beneficial for light absorption and contacting with contamination. The good combination of CQDs and UYTMs was further successfully achieved during the hydrothermal process, and demonstrated by a series of tests. The photocatalytic experiments suggested that the CQDs/UYTMs exhibited better photocatalytic activities than the pure UYTMs and P25 under both visible and UV light irradiation. The CQDs/UYTMs combining with 6 wt% of CQDs showed the best photocatalytic efficiency, while excessive CQDs tended to inhibit the photocatalytic activity. According to the results and discussions, a possible mechanism in improving the photocatalytic efficiency of the CQDs/UYTMs is significantly proposed. The up-conversion property of CQDs can broaden the absorption spectrum of CQDs/UYTMs to the visible light. Moreover, the CQDs, as the electron reservoirs, are efficient to separate the electrons and holes, leading to an improved photocatalytic activity of CQDs/UYTMs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, environmental pollution has been a serious problem around the world. More particularly, water pollution has a huge impact on human health, which is attracting considerable attention. As a very effective and promising technology, photocatalysis can efficiently promote the process of solar energy utilization, sewage disposal and environmental protection. Of all kinds of photocatalytic materials, TiO2 has been considered as one of the best candidate for the photodegradation of organic pollutants and toxic substances because of its abundance, chemically and biologically stability, high intrinsic catalytic activity, non-toxicity and environmental friendliness [1–3].

Nevertheless, the application of TiO2 is still limited by the weak visible light response due to its wide band gap (3.2 eV). Moreover, the high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs further inhibits the photocatalytic efficiency. In order to solve these problems, various solutions have been proposed, such as loading noble metals [4,5], doping metal and nonmetal [6,7], coupling with narrow bandgap semiconductors [8–10], and so on. Despite this, there are still many obstacles existing in those methods. The noble metal deposition is very expensive; the doping methods usually involve in changing the crystal structure, such as insertion of atoms, replacement of existing atoms and crystal transformation, resulting in the instability of the semiconductor; the narrow band gap semiconductors that can be used to couple with TiO2 are often toxic, difficult to synthesize or likely to arise the photocorrosion due to the unsteady structures. Therefore, it is of great significance to search for novel routes for TiO2 to further enhance the utilization efficiency of the visible light and dissociation of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs.

Nowadays, compounding TiO2 with carbonaceous materials like carbon nanotube [11,12], graphene [13,14], C60 [15], and CQDs [16–18] has become a research hotspot to improve its photocatalytic performance. CQDs are a new-type carbon-based material that have attracted intensive attention benefiting from their outstanding properties, including high chemical and optical stability, inexpensive cost, biocompatibility and low toxicity. Many articles about CQDs have been reported with emphasis on their applications in fluorescence sensor, electrochemical analysis, electrocatalysis, solar cell, fuel cell and drug carrier [19–22]. In recent years, the application of CQDs in the photocatalysis area has attracting more and more attention due to their unique electron storage and transport properties, as well as the up-conversion photoluminescence [23]. In particular, the CQDs/TiO2 composites are expected to exhibit the high visible-light photocatalytic properties. For example, Yu et al. [24] reported a moderate one-step hydrothermal process to synthesize the CQDs decorated P25 photocatalytic materials. Compared with pure P25, this CQDs/P25 composite showed enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution under both UV–Vis and visible light irradiation. Pan et al. [25] successfully loaded the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) onto anatase TiO2 nanoparticles through a hydrothermal method. The GQDs/TiO2 exhibited much better photocatalytic performance for the reason that the GQDs extended the photoresponse of TiO2 from UV to a broad visible region.

In addition, apart from the influence of light response and recombination of electron-hole pairs, the size, surface area and morphology of TiO2 also have a significant effect on the photocatalytic activity. TiO2 powders with smaller particle size generally exhibits larger specific surface area, which can promote the contact with organic contaminants and consequently improve the photocatalytic activity. However, the small particles still have some problems, such as serious aggregation, bad dispersion stability and difficulty to be separated and recycled.

In comparison, the synthesis of hierarchical TiO2 structures assembled by small nanosheets and nanowires is a more promising way to solve these problem, since they have good dispersion and stability during the cyclic reaction, and also show higher photocatalytic activity owing to their increased specific surface area [26,27]. In particularly, according to our previous study [28], the three-dimensional urchin-like and yolk-shell TiO2 microspheres with internal cavity and large surface area can significantly facilitate the light absorption and enlarge the contact area with contaminants. After further combining with CQDs, the special hierarchical structures of CQDs/UYTMs has great potential to promote the visible light-driven photocatalytic performance. Despite the high practical application value, synthesis of hierarchical CQDs/UYTMs structures has received limited attention and few related articles have been reported. Therefore, it is theoretically and practically significant to synthesize this novel material and investigate its photocatalytic activity.

In this article，a novel 3D CQDs/UYTMs were obtained through a facile hydrothermal process. The properties of CQDs, UYTMs and CQDs/UYTMs composites were systematically investigated by a series of characterization methods. The experimental results indicated that CQDs were successfully coupled with UYTMs, and increased photocatalytic activity were obtained under both UV and visible light irradiation. More importantly, a possible mechanism of improving the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs/UYTMs was significantly put forwards.

Sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4, 99%), sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O, 99%), tetrabutyl titanate (TBT, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%) and potassium chloride (KCl, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin. Ethanol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent CO., Ltd. High purity graphite rods (99.99%) were purchased from Beijing Jixingshengan Technology CO., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received.

The CQDs were carried out by electrochemical method, as shown in Fig. 1. In brief, high purity graphite rods were used as both anode and cathode, and the phosphate buffer saline (containing 8.667 g/L NaCl, 1.7178 g/L Na2HPO4 and 1.0452 g/L NaH2PO4·2H2O) was used as electrolyte. The process lasted for 48 h under cyclic voltammetric method with the voltage range of −3–3 V (voltage change rate at 0.05 V/s). With the corrosion of graphite electrode, the color of the solution changed from transparent to brownish yellow. After that, the large carbon particles in the obtained solution were removed using a 0.22 µm syringe filter. Finally, the solution were transferred to a dialysis membrane with molecular interception of 3500 Da for the dialysis treatment for 3days. The purified solution was further used to synthesize the CQDs/UYTMs composites.

The UYTMs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method as shown in Fig. 1. Firstly, the TiO2 microspheres as a precursor of UYTMs were synthesized through a sol-gel method. In a typical process, a mixed solution containing 500 mL of ethanol, 1.5 g 12-hydroxystearate and 3.8 mL of 0.073 M KCl aqueous solution were prepared. Then 30 mL of TBT was added in the above solution under magnetic stirring at 30 °C, and the mixture was stirred at 500 rpm for 18 h. The formed white precipitation was centrifuged and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and then it was dried at 40 °C overnight to obtain the TiO2 precursor microspheres. Secondly, the urchin-like and yolk-shell TiO2 microspheres were prepared by an alkaline-assisted hydrothermal method. 0.1 g of the monodispersed TiO2 precursor microspheres were added into a 50 mL aqueous solution of 0.5 M NaOH. Then, the suspensions were sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and held at 150 °C for 12 h. The precipitate was recovered by centrifugation and washed with 0.08 M HCl aqueous solution and deionized water for several times until the pH approached 7.0. After that, the precipitate was dried at 40 °C overnight, and further thermally treated at 500 °C for 5 h to obtain the crystalline UYTMs powders.

As shown in Fig. 1, a certain volume of as-prepared CQDs solution was diluted to final volume of 80 mL by deionized water. The UYTMs was then dispersed in the above solution by stirring for 1 h, then HCl aqueous solution were used to adjust the pH value approached 3.5. The resulting suspension was kept in an autoclave at 150 °C for 12 h. Finally, the CQDs/UYTMs composites were harvested after being washed and dried at 40 °C overnight.

In order to investigate the effect of different content of CQDs on the degradation efficiency, a series experiments were made by comparative method. 10.20 mL, 20.85 mL, 31.90 mL and 43.50 mL of CQDs solution (0.2 mg/mL) were bonded with 0.1 g UYTMs, forming the CQDs/UYTMs composites with 2 wt%, 4 wt%, 6 wt% and 8 wt% CQDs, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the TiO2 precursor microspheres were carried out with a Hitachi S4800. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100F electron microscope operating at 300 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was conducted on a Bruker AXS D8 with a Cu Kα irradiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) at a scanning rate of 5°/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was examined by using a Thermo Fisher ESC ALAB 250Xi with monochromatic Al Kα radiation. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer and Raman scattering was acquired on a Horiba Jobin Yvon (LabRAM), including a 532.8 nm excitation laser.

The photocatalytic test was investigated by the phtodegradation of aqueous phenol solution (15 mg/L) and methylene blue solution (15 mg/L) under visible light and UV light irradiation, respectively. A typical experiment was achieved in a 250 mL flask, including 0.1 g of photocatalyst and 100 mL of phenol solution (or methylene blue solution). The mixture was stirred in dark for 2 h before the irradiation to establish an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. A 180 mW/cm2 of Xenon lamp was used both as the UV source and the visible light source with a 400 nm cut-off filter and a 420–780 nm filter, respectively. 7 mL of suspension was collected periodically from the flask and detected by the UV–vis spectrophotometer for its residual contaminant concentration after centrifugation.

The morphology of obtained CQDs was firstly characterized by TEM as shown in Fig. 2a. It is observed that the particle size of CQDs is about 5 nm without significantly aggregations. As show in Fig. 2b, the HRTEM image reveals that the interplanar spacing of CQDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystalline face of graphitic carbon. The results preliminarily exhibit a good crystallization property of CQDs.

The structure of the CQDs were further detected by using the Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3, the Raman spectrum exhibits two characteristic peaks at 1590 cm−1 (G band) and 1350 cm−1 (D band). In general, the D-band shows the defects of graphitic materials, and the G-band presents the in-plan vibration of sp2 carbon atom [29,30]. The obvious intensity of the G-band further indicates the high crystallinity of the prepared CQDs. Based on the TEM images and Raman spectrum, it can be clearly concluded that CQDs with good crystallization property were successfully prepared by the electrochemical method.

The optical properties of the as-prepared CQDs were further explored. Figs. 4a and 4b show the optical images of CQDs illuminated by white and UV light (at 360 nm), respectively. The CQDs exhibit strong ultraviolet fluorescence properties, which can be clearly seen by the naked eyes. The photoluminescence (PL) under different excitation wavelengths was further measured and shown in Figs. 4c and 4d. As observed from Fig. 4c, the PL intensity shows a decreasing trend as the excited wavelengths increases from 280 nm to 420 nm. In addition, the PL spectra show a strong peak centered at ~480 nm without obvious shift under the excitation range of 280–360 nm. However, with the excitation wavelength increasing from 360 nm to 420 nm, the PL peaks shift to longer wavelengths. According to the previous investigation [31,32], the red-shift of PL peaks and the decreasing intensity with the increase of excitation wavelength can be explained by the gradual evolution of the confinement in quantum dots. The above phenomenon suggests the excitation-dependent PL behavior of CQDs, like many luminescent carbon nanoparticles [33]. When the excitation wavelengths ranging from 700–880 nm, as shown in Fig. 4d, the CQDs exhibit significant up-converted PL properties. The PL spectra show a strong peak centered at 488 nm with the excitation wavelengths of 700–800 nm, and shift to long wavelengths as the excitation wavelength further increasing to 880 nm. The results indicate that the up-conversion PL spectra of CQDs display the similar tunable excitation wavelength-dependent emissions performance. The up-converted property further suggest that the CQDs can absorb the NIR light and visible light, emitting the shorter wavelength light. This is to say, after compounding TiO2 and CQDs, it is expected that the broad spectrum of visible light can be used to excite TiO2 through the up-conversion process of CQDs.

Fig. 5 shows the SEM and TEM images of the TiO2 precursor microspheres and UYTMs. As observed from Fig. 5a, the TiO2 precursor microspheres show uniform sphericity and narrow diameter distribution. After the hydrothermal treatment (Fig. 5b), some lamellar structure grew on the surface, forming the UYTMs with hierarchical sea urchin-like and yolk-shell structures. The morphology and crystal structure of the UYTMs were further investigated by TEM. As show in Fig. 5c, the UYTMs present an average diameter of about 500 nm, and a significant yolk-shell morphology with large specific surface area. The mechanism for forming the distinctive structures is Ostwald ripening [28,34]. The inner cores increasingly dissolve and recrystallize on the surface of the TiO2 precursor microspheres as the hydrothermal reaction progress. And the sea urchin-like and yolk-shell structure is ultimately formed under proper reaction temperature and time. It has been demonstrated that this particular TiO2 structures are beneficial to enhance the photocatalytic activity owing to the countless active sites, increased adsorption ability and controllable light absorption property [34]. In addition, the HRTEM images in Fig. 5d further show that the lattice spacing was around 0.35 nm, which entirely consistent with the (101) crystallographic plane of anatase TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs/UYTMs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs and UYTMs are combined by hydrothermal method to form the CQDs/UYTMs. As show in Fig. 6a, the CQDs/UYTMs maintain the urchin-like and yolk shell structure. The enlarge images in Figs. 6b and 6c show that the CQDs distribute evenly throughout the surface of the UYTMs. So the good combination between the CQDs and UYTMs is preliminarily illustrated.

Fig. 7 presents the XRD patterns of the UYTMs and CQDs/UYTMs. Both of the samples show the same diffraction peaks located at the 2θ of 25.28°, 37.80°, 48.05°, 53.89° and 55.06°, corresponding to the (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5) and (2 1 1) crystal planes of anatase TiO2. This result strongly proves that the anatase phase of titanium oxide has not changed during the hydrothermal compositing process. Additionally, no XRD patterns of carbon are observed from the CQDs/UYTMs samples, which is mainly due to the tiny particle size, small amount and weak diffraction of CQDs in the composites.

It is generally believed that the Raman scattering is more sensitive than the X-ray diffraction for the structure characterization of carbon materials. Therefore, the CQDs/UYTMs were analyzed with Raman spectroscopy as well. As show in Fig. 8, the obvious peaks at 144 cm−1, 399 cm−1, 516 cm−1 and 639 cm−1 are consistent with the Eg(1), B1 g, A1 g+B1 g(2) and Eg(3) modes of anatase TiO2. This crystal structure is consistent with the XRD discussion. Moreover, the additional peaks at 1370 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 are corresponding to the D-band and G-band of the carbon structures, further demonstrating the successful combination of CQDs with anatase TiO2 in the CQDs/UYTMs.

The chemical composition and surface chemical states of CQDs/UYTMs were detected using the XPS measurement. As show in Fig. 9a, the full-survey spectrum shows strong signals of Ti 2p, C 1s and O 1s, suggesting that CQDs/UYTMs are composed of Ti, O and C elements. As shown in Fig. 9b, the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s indicates that four different chemical states exist in the carbon atoms. One of the peaks located at 284.9 eV belongs to the contaminant carbon adsorbed on the surface of the CQDs, and the other peaks at 288.5 eV, 286.0 eV and 284.6 eV are associated with C˭O, C-O and C-C/C˭C (graphite) bonds, respectively, which are well consistent with the structure of carbon quantum dots. Fig. 9c further shows the spectrum of O 1s, and the peaks at 530.7 eV, 530.1 eV and 529.6 eV are attributed to the C-O-H, C˭O and T = O bonds. The peaks of Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 9d) at 464.3 eV and 458.6 eV are assigned to Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, respectively, indicating the Ti4+ exists in the TiO2. No peaks of Ti-C bond can be found in the C 1s and Ti 2p spectra, indicating that no carbon has doped into TiO2 and the crystal structure of the anatase TiO2 has no change [35]. The conclusion further proves that the CQDs are bonded with TiO2 in the CQDs/UYTMs, which is corresponded with the HRTEM and Raman results.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQDs/UYTMs and pure UYTMs were detected to determine the separation rates of the electrons and holes [36]. Since the generation of PL is associated with the combination of the electron-hole pairs, a low PL intensity indicates a decreased recombination of the electron-hole pairs and less defects. As shown in Fig. 10, the CQDs/UYTMs exhibit much weaker emission intensity than the pure UYTMs at the same testing conditions. It can be strongly indicated that CQDs can be the acceptors and transporters of photo-generated electrons, which can effectively promote the separation of photoelectrons from vacancies and improve the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/UYTMs.

The photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/UYTMs with different content of CQDs were evaluated by the degradation of methylene blue and phenol under UV light and visible light irradiation, and the results were shown in Fig. 11 and Fig. 12, respectively. The pure UYTMs and P25 were also examined for comparison.

As shown in Fig. 11, methylene blue shows slightly self-degradation under UV light irradiation. Almost all of CQDs/UYTMs exhibit better photocatalytic activity than P25 and pure UYTMs under UV light irradiation, suggesting the CQDs are efficient to promote the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Additionally, as the content of CQDs increases from 2 wt% to 6 wt%, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency is obviously enhanced. This is mainly because the increasing of the CQDs on the surface of the UYTMs act as the electron acceptors, which can transfer the electrons from UYTMs to CQDs and consequently improve the separation efficiency of electrons and holes. However, when higher 8 wt% CQDs are coupled with UYTMs, excessive CQDs tend to occupy the main active sits on the surface of TiO2 and prevent the O2 adsorption, resulting in a high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs and poor photocatalytic activity. The composites with 6 wt% CQDs show the best photocatalytic efficiency in all samples, and the degradation rate reaching to ~99% after 20 min of ultraviolet irradiation.

Fig. 12 further shows the photocatalytic results under visible light irradiation. The UYTMs and P25 show almost no degradation via using the visible light. By contrast, it can be obviously detected that all of CQDs/UYTMs exhibit better photocatalytic efficiency than the pure UYTMs and P25. It is evidence that the CQDs can significantly improve the visible light-driven photcatalytic activity of the UYTMs. As the content of CQDs increases from 2 wt% to 8 wt%, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CQDs/UYTMs first increases and then decreases, which shows the similar trend with the results under UV light irradiation.

Moreover, the recyclability of the CQDs/UYTMs was also evaluated and presented in Fig. 13. The photocatalytic efficiency of 6 wt%-CQDs/UYTMs in decomposing phenol was tested for four cycles under visible light irradiation. No significant changes have been observed during these cyclic reactions, which demonstrated that the CQDs/UYTMs composites are quite stable and have practical application value.

Based on the analysis and results above, a possible mechanism of visible-light photocatalytic activity over CQDs/UYTMs composites are proposed and illustrated in Fig. 14. When the CQDs/UYTMs are irradiated by visible light, the CQDs with up-conversion property can absorb the long wavelength light and emit shorter wavelength light. Thus, the shorter wavelength light with sufficiently high energies photons can excite the electrons from the valence band to the conduction band of the UYTMs and create electron-hole pairs. Then the CQDs as the acceptors and transporters of photo-generated electrons can effectively facilitate the separation of photoelectrons from vacancies and enhance photocatalytic performance of CQDs/UYTMs.

In conclusion, the CQDs/UYTMs composites were successfully prepared through the facile hydrothermal process. The CQDs with good dispersion and crystalline structure were carried out by an electrochemical method, and the unique up-conversion properties of the CQDs were investigated. The UYTMs synthesized by NaOH-assistant hydrothermal method showed internal cavity and large surface area, which could enhance the light absorption and increase the contact area with contaminants. The good combination of CQDs and UYTMs in the CQDs/UYTMs was proved by a series of tests. The photocatalytic experiments suggested the CQDs/UYTMs exhibited higher photocatalytic activities than that of pure UYTMs and P25 under both UV and visible light irradiation. The CQDs/UYTMs combining with 6 wt% of CQDs showed the best photocatalytic efficiency, while excessive CQDs tended to inhibit the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the cyclic reaction indicated the CQDs/UYTMs composites were highly stable and could be repeatedly used. Based on the above research, a possible visible light photocatalytic mechanism over CQDs/UYTMs composites was proposed. The CQDs with up-conversion property were demonstrated to extend the light adsorption of anatase TiO2 into the visible range. Moreover, the CQDs as the electron reservoirs could efficiently separate the electron-hole pairs by trapping the photo-generated electrons from the conduction band of UYTMs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0304305, 2016YFC0700607).
